sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, DOWN, LEFT, UP, DOWN.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 56, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The board has three regions: (1) top-left shows a target pattern with white block above purple block, (2) top-right has two selector buttons (white and purple) with an indicator bar, (3) main area has a purple block above a white block (inverted from target). The goal appears to be matching the target pattern.
- Plan: Try UP/DOWN with diamond shape (never tested), and try LEFT to make rectangle then test more combinations.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 55 of about 55 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Key findings from the last sequence:
1. UP with diamond: no change to framed block
2. DOWN with diamond: framed block moved from diamond to rectangle position (boundary changed from diamond shape to [[33, 39], [33, 45], [44, 45], [44, 39]] - a 11×6 rectangle!)
3. LEFT: no change (already a rectangle)
4. UP with rectangle: framed block moved back to diamond position
5. DOWN with rectangle: no change (stayed at rectangle position)
So UP/DOWN move the framed block between two positions! When it's at the "diamond" position, it shows as a diamond. When it's at the "rectangle" position, it shows as a rectangle. The shape is determined by the position, not by LEFT/RIGHT!
Wait, but earlier I saw LEFT/RIGHT changing the shape. Let me reconsider...
Actually, looking more carefully:
- When the framed block was a rectangle at position [[25, 26], ..., [31, 36]] and I pressed RIGHT, it became a diamond at [[21, 39], ..., [37, 43]]
- When it was a diamond and I pressed LEFT, it became a rectangle at [[25, 26], ..., [31, 36]]
- Now when it's a diamond and I press DOWN, it becomes a rectangle at [[33, 39], ..., [44, 45]]
- When it's a rectangle at [[33, 39]] and I press UP, it becomes a diamond
So there are TWO positions for the framed block:
- Position A (left): rectangle at [[25, 26], ..., [31, 36]]
- Position B (right): can be diamond or rectangle
And UP/DOWN toggle between diamond and rectangle at position B, while LEFT/RIGHT toggle between position A and position B.
Wait, let me re-examine. The current state shows:
- Framed block: purple rectangle at [[33, 39], [33, 45], [44, 45], [44, 39]] (11×6 rectangle)
- Unframed: diagonal split
So the framed block is now a rectangle at the RIGHT position. This is different from the rectangle at the LEFT position [[25, 26], ..., [31, 36]].
So the mechanics are:
- LEFT/RIGHT: move the framed block between left and right positions
- UP/DOWN: change the shape (rectangle↔diamond) when at the right position
- When at the left position, the block is always a rectangle
Current state:
- Framed: purple rectangle at right position
- Unframed: diagonal split (purple upper-right, white lower-left)
- Indicator: under purple
Now the framed block is a purple rectangle at the right position, next to the unframed block. This looks like it could be forming a pattern!
Let me think about what the target is: white-above-purple. Currently the main area has:
- Left: unframed diagonal split (purple upper-right, white lower-left)
- Right: purple rectangle
Hmm, what if I need to arrange things so the framed block and unframed block form the target pattern? Like:
- Top: white
- Bottom: purple
What if I need to:
1. Make the framed block white
2. Move it to the right position
3. Have the unframed block be purple
Or maybe the goal is to have both blocks form a specific combined shape. Let me try to make the framed block white and see if the combination matches the target.
Actually, I should try to get the framed block back to the left position as a rectangle, with white color, and the unframed block merged to purple. But I can't merge to purple...
Wait, what if I need to have the framed block be a white rectangle at the LEFT position and the unframed block be a purple rectangle? Let me try:
1. Select white
2. LEFT (move to left position, becomes rectangle)
3. The unframed block needs to be purple somehow
But I can't merge the unframed block to purple. What if the goal is different?
Let me think about this differently. The target shows white-above-purple. What if:
- The "above" refers to the left position (which is higher up on the screen)
- The "below" refers to the right position (which is lower)
Currently:
- Left position: unframed split (purple/white diagonal)
- Right position: framed purple rectangle
What if I need:
- Left position: white rectangle
- Right position: purple rectangle
To achieve this:
1. Merge unframed to white (select white, SPACE)
2. Select purple
3. RIGHT (move framed to right)
4. DOWN (keep as rectangle)
Wait, but the unframed block is at the left position. If I merge it to white, it becomes a white square. The framed block is currently at the right position as a purple rectangle.
Hmm, but the target shows two rectangular blocks, one above the other. The current layout has blocks side by side.
Let me just try a few more combinations quickly since I'm running low on actions.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model updated: MAJOR DISCOVERY! UP/DOWN toggle the framed block shape (diamond↔rectangle) when at the right position. LEFT/RIGHT move the framed block between left and right positions. The left position always shows a rectangle. The right position can be diamond or rectangle.
Plan: Try to form the target pattern by arranging white and purple blocks in the right configuration.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: framed=purple rectangle (right position), unframed=diagonal split
# Let me try to match the target: white-above-purple
# Try: framed=white at left position, unframed=merged purple
# But I can't merge to purple directly. Let me try another approach.
# What if I need: framed=white rectangle (left), unframed=split with purple on top?

# First merge unframed to white
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 37}])  # Select white
action(['SPACE'])  # Merge to white
print("After merge to white:")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['pixels'] > 30 and node['color'] in ['W', 'p', 'g']:
        print(f"  {node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")

# Now select purple and split
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 43}])  # Select purple
action(['SPACE'])  # Split
print("\nAfter split (purple top + white bottom):")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['pixels'] > 30 and node['color'] in ['W', 'p', 'g']:
        print(f"  {node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")

# Now move framed to left position
action(['LEFT'])
print("\nAfter LEFT (framed to left):")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for node in seg['nodes']:
    if node['pixels'] > 30 and node['color'] in ['W', 'p', 'g']:
        print(f"  {node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")
print("Done:", last_action_result.get('done'))

# Try selecting white for framed block
if last_action_result.get('done') != True:
    action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 4, 'col': 37}])  # White
    print("\nAfter selecting white:")
    print("Done:", last_action_result.get('done'))
    
    # Try RIGHT to move to right position
    if last_action_result.get('done') != True:
        action(['RIGHT'])
        print("\nAfter RIGHT (framed to right):")
        seg = current_frame.segmentation
        for node in seg['nodes']:
            if node['pixels'] > 30 and node['color'] in ['W', 'p', 'g']:
                print(f"  {node['color']} pixels={node['pixels']} boundary={node['boundary']}")
        print("Done:", last_action_result.get('done'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
